title: Advancing the green finance in the policies approach: A regime-switching evidence from world's large energy users

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: Advancing the green finance in the policies approach: A regime-switching evidence from world's large energy users

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
As an AI language model, I do not have personal views or beliefs, and I cannot provide empirical data. However, I can explain the meaning of the statement.

The statement suggests that the adoption of green finance in policies could be improved by using a regime-switching approach. This approach involves switching between different policy regimes depending on the prevailing economic conditions. The evidence presented supports the idea that this approach could be effective in promoting green finance among the world's largest energy users.

turns.speaker: user
turns.text: In this study, one can conclude that green finance (GF) is invulnerable to disruption after decision-making followed by the twenty-five large energy-consuming economies (LECE) associated with (i) energy trilemma (ET) policies, (ii) complexity-based policies, and (iii) geostrategy policies when the regime switches. Also, slight decreases in the log values of growth rate are related to a higher probability of remaining in the low GF growth regime. Notably, it decreases the transition probability value out of the “sharply downward” state and raises the transition probability value from the “sharply downward” state to the “slightly downward” state. Accordingly, the pattern of GF captured in response to the specified combination of (i) ET policies, e.g., energy equity (EE), energy security (ES), and environmental sustainability (EVS), (ii) complexity-based policies, e.g., sharing economy (SE) and economic complexity (EC), and (iii) geostrategy policies, e.g., geopolitical risk index (GPRI) and energy prices (EP), under both regimes helps to mitigate risks, develop the resilience of the energy systems, and promote GF in the short, medium, and long-run.

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text: The statement suggests that the study found that green finance (GF) is not easily disrupted when decisions are made based on policies related to energy trilemma (ET), complexity-based policies, and geostrategy policies in the 25 largest energy-consuming economies (LECE) in the world. The study also found that slight decreases in the growth rate of GF are associated with a higher probability of remaining in a low growth regime for GF. However, this also decreases the likelihood of switching from a sharply downward GF growth state to a slightly downward GF growth state. According to the study, the combination of ET policies (energy equity, energy security, and environmental sustainability), complexity-based policies (sharing economy and economic complexity), and geostrategy policies (geopolitical risk index and energy prices) can help mitigate risks, develop resilience, and promote GF in the short, medium, and long-term.
